questions.question_text: Under RSMo 339.750, dual agency in Missouri is permitted only when which condition is met?
distractors: The broker notifies MREC in advance
The transaction is under $500,000
Both parties share the same attorney
questions.explanation: RSMo 339.750.1 permits dual agency only with the consent of all parties, and consent is presumed by a written agreement under RSMo 339.780. Section 339.710 defines a dual agent as acting 'with the written consent of all parties,' and 339.780.4 requires the written dual-agency agreement before any brokerage activity. Verbal or implied consent is insufficient.

questions.question_text: Broker Martinez has written dual agency consent from both buyer and seller. She learns the buyer will accept a higher price. May she share this with the seller?
questions.correct_answer: No, unless the buyer consents to disclosure
distractors: Yes, because dual agents serve both parties
Yes, but only if the seller asks directly
No, dual agents cannot communicate at all
questions.explanation: Even with dual agency consent, a limited agent may disclose material information but must NOT reveal confidential information without the client's consent. A buyer's willingness to pay more is confidential. Martinez needs specific authorization from the buyer before sharing this.

questions.question_text: What is the legal designation of a dual agent under Missouri statute?
distractors: General agent
Transaction broker
Fiduciary agent
questions.explanation: RSMo 339.750.2 provides that a dual agent 'shall be a limited agent for both the seller and buyer or the landlord and tenant.' Note that in Missouri all statutory agents -- including single seller's and buyer's agents -- are 'limited agents' whose duties are set by statute (RSMo 339.730-339.750); what distinguishes a dual agent is that the limited agency runs to both parties at once, with the additional confidentiality restrictions of 339.750.4.

questions.question_text: A seller verbally agrees to dual agency but refuses to sign the written consent form. The broker proceeds with dual representation. What is the result?
questions.correct_answer: The broker has violated Missouri law
distractors: Verbal consent satisfies the requirement
The broker can proceed if witnessed
The violation is cured at closing
questions.explanation: Missouri requires WRITTEN consent from all parties for dual agency (RSMo 339.710 defines a dual agent as acting with the written consent of all parties, and RSMo 339.780.4 requires the written dual-agency agreement before brokerage activity). Verbal agreement is legally insufficient. By proceeding without written consent, the broker has violated Missouri's agency statutes and faces potential disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: A dual agent discovers a significant foundation defect during a showing. Both buyer and seller are the agent's clients. How should the agent handle this information?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose to both parties as a material fact
distractors: Disclose only to the buyer
Keep it confidential from both parties
Withdraw from the transaction silently
questions.explanation: Adverse material facts must be disclosed to all parties regardless of agency type. A foundation defect is a material fact, not confidential client information. As a limited agent representing both sides, the broker is obligated to ensure both buyer and seller are informed.

questions.question_text: A Missouri brokerage practices designated agency. Under the Missouri Broker Disclosure Form, which circumstance is one of the two exceptions that can result in dual agency (or transaction brokerage)?
questions.correct_answer: The same agent listed and represents the buyer
distractors: Both agents attend the same closing
The broker collects commission from both sides
The property is a new construction home
questions.explanation: The MREC Broker Disclosure Form lists two exceptions, 'with both resulting in dual agency or transaction brokerage': (1) the agent representing the buyer is also the agent who listed the property, and (2) the supervising broker of the two designated agents becomes involved in the transaction. When the same licensee is on both sides, RSMo 339.820.2 makes that licensee a dual agent or a transaction broker -- dual agency requires the written consent of all parties; otherwise the licensee defaults to transaction-broker status.

questions.question_text: Agent Lee is the designated buyer's agent and Agent Park is the designated seller's agent at the same firm. Their supervising broker becomes personally involved in the transaction. Under Missouri's designated agency rules, what results?
questions.correct_answer: Dual agency or transaction brokerage, depending on the parties' written consent
distractors: Both designated agency appointments are automatically terminated
No change, because a supervising broker is exempt from agency rules
The two designated agents become subagents of each other
questions.explanation: The supervising broker of two designated agents becoming involved in the transaction is the second exception listed on the Missouri Broker Disclosure Form, 'with both resulting in dual agency or transaction brokerage.' Under RSMo 339.820.2 a licensee on both sides of a transaction is a dual agent or a transaction broker: dual agency requires the written consent of all parties (RSMo 339.710, 339.780.4), and without that consent the broker is deemed a transaction broker and must give immediate notice of that status, confirmed in writing (RSMo 339.710(23)(c)). The designated agents themselves keep their single-agency roles.

questions.question_text: What is the primary advantage of designated agency over dual agency for consumers?
questions.correct_answer: Each client has an agent with full fiduciary duty
distractors: It eliminates the need for written consent
Commission costs are automatically reduced
The broker assumes all legal liability
questions.explanation: Designated agency allows each party to have their own agent who owes them full fiduciary duties, including undivided loyalty and advocacy. In dual agency, the broker becomes a limited agent with reduced duties to both sides. Written consent is still required for designated agency.

questions.question_text: How many exceptions can convert a designated agency arrangement into a dual agency situation in Missouri?
distractors: One exception
Three exceptions
Four exceptions
questions.explanation: The Missouri Broker Disclosure Form lists two exceptions to designated agency, 'with both resulting in dual agency or transaction brokerage': (1) the agent representing the buyer or tenant also listed the property, and (2) the supervising broker of the two designated agents becomes involved in the transaction. Which result occurs depends on consent: dual agency requires the written consent of all parties (RSMo 339.710); otherwise the licensee is deemed a transaction broker.

questions.question_text: Broker Davis designates Agent A for the seller and Agent B for the buyer. Neither exception applies. What fiduciary duties does Agent B owe the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: Full fiduciary duties to the buyer
distractors: Limited duties as a dual agent
Only disclosure of material facts
No fiduciary duties in designated agency
questions.explanation: In a properly structured designated agency where neither exception applies, each designated agent owes full fiduciary duties to their assigned client. Agent B owes the buyer complete loyalty, confidentiality, disclosure, obedience, and accounting.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is considered confidential information under Missouri agency law?
questions.correct_answer: A buyer's willingness to pay more than the price offered
distractors: A known roof leak in the property
The property's listing price history
Recent comparable sales in the area
questions.explanation: Under RSMo 339.750.4, confidential information includes a party's willingness to pay more than the price offered or accept less than the asking price, motivating factors, willingness to agree to other financing terms, and the terms of any prior offers or counteroffers. Property defects are adverse material facts that must be disclosed, and listing histories and comparable sales are not confidential client information.

questions.question_text: A seller tells her agent she is motivated to sell quickly due to a job relocation. A buyer's agent asks the listing agent why the seller is selling. What should the listing agent say?
questions.correct_answer: Decline to share the seller's motivation
distractors: Disclose the relocation as a material fact
Share the information to facilitate the deal
Reveal it only if the buyer makes an offer
questions.explanation: Motivating factors are confidential client information under Missouri law (RSMo 339.750.4(3) for dual agents; for a listing agent, the confidentiality duty of RSMo 339.730.2 and the duty of loyalty and utmost good faith reach the same result). The listing agent must not reveal the seller's urgency due to relocation, as this could weaken the seller's negotiating position. Disclosure requires the seller's consent.

questions.question_text: Missouri's confidentiality obligations for client information apply to which agency relationships?
distractors: Only dual agency
Only seller's and buyer's agency
Only designated agency
questions.explanation: Confidentiality obligations regarding client information -- including willingness to pay more or accept less and motivating factors -- apply across all Missouri brokerage relationships: seller's agent, buyer's agent, dual agent, designated agent, and the transaction broker (which is statutorily a non-agency, neutral relationship but carries the same confidentiality duties under RSMo 339.755.3).

questions.question_text: Agent Patel's buyer client says she will pay up to $400,000 but wants to start at $350,000. The seller's agent asks Patel about the buyer's maximum budget. What must Patel do?
questions.correct_answer: Refuse to disclose the buyer's maximum
distractors: Disclose if it will help close the deal
Share the $400,000 figure confidentially
Reveal it only after an accepted offer
questions.explanation: A buyer's willingness to pay more than the offered amount is expressly confidential under Missouri law. Agent Patel must refuse to share the $400,000 maximum regardless of pressure from the other side. Only the buyer can authorize disclosure of this information.

questions.question_text: A former client's willingness to accept a lower price was shared by the broker after the agency relationship ended. The client files a complaint. Is the broker liable?
distractors: No, duties end when the agreement expires
No, because pricing information is never confidential
Yes, but only within 90 days of termination
questions.explanation: Under RSMo 339.790.2(2), the duty to keep client confidences -- such as willingness to accept a lower price -- survives termination of the brokerage relationship. Post-termination disclosure requires the client's written consent (other narrow exceptions: information required to be disclosed by law, made public by the client or another source, or needed in the broker's self-defense). None applies here, so the client's complaint is valid.
